 spanner_emulator() {
+  local dialect=${1:-GOOGLE_STANDARD_SQL}

+    DB_COUNT=4

+  for n in $(seq 1 ${DB_COUNT}); do
+    local container_name="spanner_${n}"

-      echo "Cloud Spanner emulator started."
-      break
+  # Wait for all emulators to be ready
+  for n in $(seq 1 ${DB_COUNT}); do
+    local container_name="spanner_${n}"
+    local port=$((9010 + n))
+    
+    local retries=0
+    until [ "$retries" -ge 20 ]; do
+      local logs="$($CONTAINER_CLI logs ${container_name} 2>&1 || true)"
+      if [[ "$logs" == *"gRPC server listening"* ]] || [[ "$logs" == *"Cloud Spanner emulator running"* ]]; then
+        echo "Cloud Spanner emulator (${container_name}) started on port ${port}."
+        break
+      fi
+      echo "Waiting for Cloud Spanner emulator (${container_name}) to start..."
+      retries=$((retries+1))
+      sleep 3
+    done
+    
+    if [ "$retries" -ge 20 ]; then
+       echo "Cloud Spanner emulator (${container_name}) failed to start"
+       exit 1
     fi
-    echo "Waiting for Cloud Spanner emulator to start..."
-    n=$((n+1))
-    sleep 3

-    exit 1
-  fi
+  # Configure Instance

+              "nodeCount": 1
+            }

POINTS:
- [docker_db.sh:1582] Based on the Gradle script changes, it seems to me that this would be more appropriate.

```suggestion
  if [[ $DB_COUNT > 8 ]]; then
    DB_COUNT=4
  else
    DB_COUNT=$(( DB_COUNT / 2 ))
  fi
```
- [hibernate-testing/src/main/java/org/hibernate/testing/orm/junit/DialectFeatureChecks.java:184] ```suggestion
			return !"none".equals( dialect.getDefaultProperties().get( MappingSettings.PREFERRED_POOLED_OPTIMIZER ) );
```
- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/SpannerPostgreSQLDialect.java:191] Note that the second argument is a function name as registered in the `FunctionRegistry`, not a native SQL function name, so this is just setting up an alias in order for result types to be correct. Please apply the suggestion I made or explain why it doesn't work.
- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/SpannerPostgreSQLDialect.java:372] In `Dialect` there is this call `ddlTypeRegistry.addDescriptor( simpleSqlType( BIGINT ) );`, which will add the DDL type for the BIGINT type code and the `bigint` sql type name to the `DdlTypeRegistry`.
`Dialect#resolveSqlTypeCode()` will then call `ddlTypeRegistry.getSqlTypeCode()` which will find that descriptor. I don't understand why this would be necessary, so please post the test that fails when this line is missing. Something else must be going on IMO.

```

1. Automatic detection order now includes Jackson 3.
JSON order is now Jackson 2 with OSON, Jackson 2, Jackson 3, Jakarta JSON B.
XML order is now Jackson 2, Jackson 3, JAXB.
2. OSON doesn't support Jackson 3 right now, so it isn't included.
3. There is duplicate code between the Jackson 2 and Jackson 3 support. There could likely be some refactoring done to reduce this if that's desired (either in this PR or a separate one).
4. I didn't test this in a separate project or anything, just `./gradlew clean build`.

reference_review:
SUMMARY:
Hi @TomasHofman I have not finished the review of this, there is still work to do here.

The main issue is that the installer hisgh level command (and the Web Console as well) should be backward compatible with legacy host controllers. We should even be able to continue testing it against legacy management operations, so probably we will need to do additional adjustments on TestInstallationManager to continue covering the legacy results.

It would be better to discuss how we evolve this in Zulip instead of in this PR, I've just added some details at a high level, but we are going to need a different approach to continue evolving these operations across multiple host controller versions.

POINTS:
- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/cli/ListManifestVersionsCommand.java:89] Commands are the commands exposed to the CLI. Commands could have actions, which allow the execution of multiple management operations by a single command in different stages. 

For example, the UpdateCommand is executed in two stages, first it uses the `ListUpdatesAction` to get the list of available updates; second, it uses the PrepareUpdateAction to prepare the update.

Actions are not exposed to the CLI; they are discrete pieces that can be shared and reused by the commands.

The general mechanic is:
1. Design your command by extending from `AbstractInstMgrCommand`.
2. Override the buildOperation if your command generates a management operation. Inthat method you have to build the final management operation that this command is going to execute
3. Override the execute method, to validate the command arguments
4. Invoke the executeOp to trigger it.


Here, `ListManifestVersionsAction` is unnecessary, and all the logic there could be assumed by the `ListManifestVersionsCommand` instead, since there is a single command. We do not need an action since there is a one-to-one relationship between the `list-manifest-versions` high level command and the low-level management operation it triggers.

`ListManifestVersionsAction`  can be removed, and its logic should be assumed by the `ListManifestVersionsCommand`, otherwise it will introduce additional unnecessary complexity.
- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/cli/ListManifestVersionsCommand.java:111] I think with the other explanation about Command vs Action the utility of buildOperation method gets clearer.

It will be invoked to build the Operation object when the management operation is about to be executed.

In this case, you are never invoking `this.executeOp(ctx, this.host);`, basically because you have addedan action to do that, so this method will never be invoked here. 

For those cases, we could override it as:

```
@Override
    protected Operation buildOperation() {
        throw new IllegalStateException("Update Command has not build operation");
    }
```

However, that would be the preference if your command is composed of multiple actions. For this PR, it is better to get rid of the `ListManifestVersionsAction` since it will add unnecessary complexity.
- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/cli/UpdateCommand.java:120] @TomasHofman These are not backward compatibility changes. The high-level command should be able to manage responses coming from legacy host controllers as well since it shouldbe possible to continue using it to patch legcy host controllers. 

The Web Console should do the same since the Web Console should be able to continue using the data returned by legacy hosts. CC @hpehl
- [installation-manager/src/test/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/cli/ListManifestVersionsCommandTestCase.java:1] Missing license headers, but in any case, we don't do unit tests for the installation manage high level command, it is ok if you want to add them, but I will remove them and assert the cases via integration tests.

We do unit tests for management resource operations, and high-level command tests are integration tests and should be placed at:

standalone -> org/jboss/as/test/manualmode/installationmanager/InstallationManagerIntegrationTestCase.java

Domain Mode -> org/jboss/as/test/integration/domain/installationmanager/InstallationManagerIntegrationTestCase.java
- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/cli/ListManifestVersionsCommand.java:40] Any new command should be documented at src/main/resources/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/cli/command_resources.properties

SUMMARY:
Managing the life cycle of the server in the test suite is a bit tricky sometimes, and it could influence on other tests, so I've added some comments about what I think could mitigate it

POINTS:
- [security-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/extension/security/manager/SecurityManagerSubsystemAdd.java:67] optional, null assignements can be removed, those variables will never be used without being initialized, so can be just:

```
        ModelNode minimumPermissionsNode;
        ModelNode maximumPermissionsNode;
```
- [testsuite/manualmode/src/test/java/org/jboss/as/test/manualmode/secman/UnresolvedExpressionSecurityManagerTestCase.java:127] I think you will need to manage two cases here:

1. If sec mgr was enabled, this reload will end up in a server stopped.
In this case, you can send the reload operation, assert that the reload operation was executed succesfully and then call the container.stop(), to ensure it waits for the process to be fully stopped.

2. If sec mgr is not enabled, the reload will success.
So, in this case, you should be able to use `ServerReload.executeReloadAndWaitForCompletion(client, false);`
